explicit_text: I was born in Long Prairie, Minnesota on January 30, 1946, and lived there for about six years. My dad owned a clothing store in town, a small family business. It was a quiet place, but eventually my family decided to move to Colorado because my dad loved hunting mule deer there.
implicit_text: I was born in a small Minnesota town on January 30, 1946, and lived there for about six years. My dad owned a clothing store in that quiet community, a small family business. Eventually, my family decided to move west because my dad loved hunting mule deer in the mountains.

explicit_text: My family moved to Colorado in 1952 after my dad, who loved mule deer hunting there, decided it was time for a change. We lived in Denver for a year, then Aurora for another, and finally settled in Boulder until 1960. Boulder was a small, idyllic town of about 25,000 people where I spent much of my childhood climbing the flatirons and enjoying the outdoors.
implicit_text: My family moved west in 1952 after my dad, who loved mule deer hunting in the Rocky Mountains, decided it was time for a change. We lived in a major city there for a year, then a nearby suburb for another, and finally settled in a small college town until 1960. That town, with about 25,000 people, was where I spent much of my childhood climbing local rock formations and enjoying the outdoors.

explicit_text: When we first moved to Colorado in 1952, we lived in Denver for about a year. It was part of the transition period before we moved to Aurora and then Boulder. Denver was just the starting point of our family's new life in Colorado.

explicit_text: After living in Denver for a year, my family moved to Aurora, Colorado, where we stayed for another year before settling in Boulder. It was part of the gradual move westward that my parents planned. Aurora was a brief but important stop in our Colorado years.
implicit_text: After living in the big city for a year, my family moved to a nearby suburb where we stayed for another year before settling in the college town. It was part of the gradual move westward that my parents planned. That suburb was a brief but important stop in our mountain state years.

explicit_text: We moved to Boulder in the mid-1950s and stayed there until 1960. My dad bought a motel on the outskirts of town near the Boulder River, and I spent a lot of time climbing the flatirons. Boulder was a small, desegregated town with about 25,000 people, and it was a very idyllic place to grow up.
implicit_text: We moved to a small college town in the mid-1950s and stayed there until 1960. My dad bought a motel on the outskirts near a local river, and I spent a lot of time climbing the famous nearby rock formations. That town was small, desegregated, with about 25,000 people, and it was a very idyllic place to grow up.

explicit_text: In 1960, my family moved to Paris, Texas, which was a major culture shock after Boulder. The town was still segregated when I attended high school there, which was very different from the desegregated environment I was used to. I was in the last graduating class before desegregation, and the racial attitudes in Paris were stark, even with signs boasting about being 'the whitest people.'
implicit_text: In 1960, my family moved to a Texas town that was a major culture shock after the mountain college town. The place was still segregated when I attended high school there, very different from the desegregated environment I was used to. I was in the last graduating class before desegregation, and the racial attitudes were stark, even with signs boasting about being 'the whitest people.'

explicit_text: Driving through Greenville, Texas, on the way to Dallas, I remember seeing a huge sign that said 'Welcome to Greenville, home of the blackest soil and the whitest people.' It was a striking example of the racial attitudes in East Texas during the 1960s, and the sign stayed up even after federal laws changed. It really highlighted the cultural divide I experienced living in that region.
implicit_text: Driving through a Texas town on the way to Dallas, I remember seeing a huge sign that said 'Welcome to [that town], home of the blackest soil and the whitest people.' It was a striking example of the racial attitudes in East Texas during the 1960s, and the sign stayed up even after federal laws changed. It really highlighted the cultural divide I experienced living in that region.

explicit_text: My younger brother, who is two years younger than me, currently lives in Provo, Utah. While I didn't spend much time there growing up, it's where he settled later in life. It's interesting to think about how our paths diverged geographically after our childhoods.

explicit_text: Although I had no formal training or close friends involved, I was aware of the Civil Rights Movement through newspapers, even if the local Paris Daily News wasn't very sympathetic. I reacted personally when I found out my dad was segregating motel rooms by race, marking black guests with a 'C' on registration cards. I would secretly write 'W' on the other cards to counteract that, which was my small way of responding to racial injustice at the time.

I reacted personally when I found out my dad was segregating motel rooms by race, marking black guests with a 'C' on registration cards. I would secretly write 'W' on the other cards to counteract that, which was my small way of responding to racial injustice at the time.

explicit_text: In high school, my friends and I formed folk groups that were heavily influenced by the Smothers Brothers. We did straight-up covers of their material and even put on variety shows and assemblies inspired by their style. They were a big influence on our early music performances and helped shape our act.
implicit_text: In high school, my friends and I formed folk groups that were heavily influenced by a popular comedic folk duo. We did straight-up covers of their material and even put on variety shows and assemblies inspired by their style. They were a big influence on our early music performances and helped shape our act.

explicit_text: During my time at Paris Junior College, along with my bandmates, we discovered Bob Dylan and other artists who were electrifying folk music. Dylan's music was part of the soundtrack to our early rock 'n' roll band, and his influence helped us transition from acoustic to electric instruments. His work was a big part of the musical and cultural environment we were immersed in during the late 1960s.

His music was part of the soundtrack to our early rock 'n' roll band, and his influence helped us transition from acoustic to electric instruments. His work was a big part of the musical and cultural environment we were immersed in during the late 1960s.

explicit_text: After Paris Junior College, my band and I moved to Commerce, Texas, to attend East Texas State University. We kept the band together and played gigs every weekend while I pursued my degree. It was a time filled with music, some political exposure, and a lot of youthful energy despite the distractions.
implicit_text: After junior college, my band and I moved to a Texas town known for its university to continue our studies. explicit_text: One of the most political songs we performed was Country Joe and the Fish's 'Fixin' to Die Rag,' which was about the Vietnam War. It was probably the most political act we did musically during college, reflecting some of the growing awareness and unrest around the war. That song connected us to the broader countercultural movement of the time.
implicit_text: One of the most political songs we performed was a Vietnam War protest anthem by a psychedelic rock band. It was probably the most political act we did musically during college, reflecting some of the growing awareness and unrest around the war. That song connected us to the broader countercultural movement of the time.

explicit_text: Some of my college friends connected with the Students for a Democratic Society from Austin, who came to Commerce to put on political festivals and roadshows. Despite initial fears about hostility from local rednecks, the events were surprisingly successful and even led to friendships between us and some of the locals. SDS introduced me to a more radical political perspective during that time.
implicit_text: Some of my college friends connected with a prominent left-wing activist group from a Texas capital city, who came to our college town to put on political festivals and roadshows. Despite initial fears about hostility from local rednecks, the events were surprisingly successful and even led to friendships between us and some of the locals. This group introduced me to a more radical political perspective during that time.

explicit_text: Austin was the home base for the Students for a Democratic Society members who came out to Commerce to organize political events. It was a center of radical politics that contrasted sharply with East Texas. Their presence in Austin helped bring new ideas and activism to my college town.

explicit_text: I attended Paris Junior College in Paris, Texas, where most of my friends also went. It was there that we formed one of the first rock 'n' roll bands in East Texas and started experimenting with electric guitars. The college years were formative for both my music and my early political exposure.

explicit_text: In September 1968, I found myself at Fort Polk, Louisiana, for basic training after joining the Army. I was older and whiter than most of the other recruits, many of whom had been drafted or chosen the Army over jail. It was a tough environment, and I quickly realized I didn't belong or want to lead others into a war I was uneasy about.
implicit_text: In September 1968, I found myself at a Louisiana Army base for basic training after joining the military. I was older and whiter than most of the other recruits, many of whom had been drafted or chosen the Army over jail. It was a tough environment, and I quickly realized I didn't belong or want to lead others into a war I was uneasy about.

explicit_text: The Vietnam War loomed large during my college and Army years. I joined the Army in 1968 without strong moral objections but became increasingly conflicted during training. The war was the subject of protest songs we played, and I eventually explored conscientious objection and other ways to avoid combat deployment to Vietnam.
implicit_text: The conflict in Southeast Asia loomed large during my college and Army years. I joined the military in 1968 without strong moral objections but became increasingly conflicted during training. The war was the subject of protest songs we played, and I eventually explored conscientious objection and other ways to avoid combat deployment to that region.

explicit_text: When I joined the Army, I was told I would be assigned to the Adjutant General's Corps, which meant easy duty like attending embassy parties in D.C. I was naive and believed I wouldn't see combat, but that turned out to be false. I ended up in infantry training and quickly realized that path was not for me.
implicit_text: When I joined the military, I was told I would be assigned to a support corps responsible for administrative duties, which meant easy duty like attending embassy parties in the capital. I was naive and believed I wouldn't see combat, but that turned out to be false. I ended up in infantry training and quickly realized that path was not for me.

explicit_text: When faced with deployment orders to Vietnam, I learned about conscientious objection from a friend connected to the SDS. It was the only legal way to avoid combat, though it was a difficult process. This knowledge gave me and others options beyond desertion or refusing orders, and it became a crucial part of how I navigated my Army experience.
implicit_text: When faced with deployment orders to Southeast Asia, I learned about the legal option to refuse combat service on moral grounds from a friend connected to the activist group. It was the only legal way to avoid combat, though it was a difficult process. This knowledge gave me and others options beyond desertion or refusing orders, and it became a crucial part of how I navigated my military experience.

explicit_text: During the crisis of being shipped off to Vietnam, some of us considered deserting and going to Canada as an alternative to fighting. It was one of the options discussed alongside conscientious objection and refusing orders. Though risky, Canada represented a potential refuge for those opposed to the war.
implicit_text: During the crisis of being shipped off to Southeast Asia, some of us considered deserting and going to a neighboring northern country as an alternative to fighting. It was one of the options discussed alongside conscientious objection and refusing orders. Though risky, that country represented a potential refuge for those opposed to the war.
